news_summary: Today's health news highlights breakthroughs in understanding and treating brain inflammation and hepatitis B, alongside promising benefits of intermittent fasting on brain function and innovative animal-assisted therapy improving psychiatric care.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité médicale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans la compréhension et le traitement de l'inflammation cérébrale et de l'hépatite B, ainsi que les bienfaits prometteurs du jeûne intermittent sur les fonctions cérébrales et une thérapie innovante assistée par des animaux qui améliore les soins psychiatriques.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de salud de hoy destacan los avances en la comprensión y el tratamiento de la inflamación cerebral y la hepatitis B, además de los prometedores beneficios del ayuno intermitente para la función cerebral y una innovadora terapia asistida con animales que mejora la atención psiquiátrica.

articles.title: Scientists found the hidden switch fueling alzheimer’s brain inflammation
articles.summary: Scientists at Scripps Research have uncovered a molecular “switch” that appears to fuel the damaging brain inflammation seen in Alzheimer’s disease. They found that a protein called STING becomes chemically altered in a way that keeps the brain’s immune system stuck in overdrive, harming the connections between nerve cells.
articles.content:
The brain has its own built in immune defenses that help detect threats and protect nerve cells. But growing evidence suggests that in Alzheimer's disease, these immune cells become stuck in a state of chronic activation. Instead of helping, they trigger ongoing inflammation that can damage the connections between brain cells.

Now, researchers at Scripps Research have identified a molecular mechanism that appears to play a key role in that process. Using human Alzheimer's brain cells and other experimental models, the team discovered a chemical change that can push the brain's immune response into overdrive. The findings, published in Cell Chemical Biology, point to a promising new target for future Alzheimer's treatments.

A Key Protein Linked to Brain Inflammation

The study focuses on a protein called STING, which normally serves as part of the body's early warning system against threats. Researchers found that in Alzheimer's disease, STING undergoes a chemical modification known as S-nitrosylation (or SNO, a reaction involving sulfur, oxygen and nitrogen). This alteration appears to make the protein excessively active, fueling harmful inflammation.

When the scientists blocked this specific chemical modification in a mouse model of Alzheimer's disease, levels of neuroinflammation dropped.

"This is a new and important therapeutic target for Alzheimer's disease," says senior author Stuart Lipton, the Step Family Foundation Endowed Chair at Scripps Research and a clinical neurologist. "It's exciting to see that blocking this switch in mice reduces inflammation and protects the very brain cell connections that are lost in Alzheimer's, especially because we found the same pathway to be activated in human Alzheimer's brain samples and in human stem cell-derived models."

The Discovery of a Harmful Chemical Process

More than 30 years ago, Lipton discovered the biological process known as S-nitrosylation. During this reaction, a molecule related to nitric oxide (NO) attaches to a cysteine amino acid within a protein, creating what scientists call "SNO" and altering the protein's behavior.

Previous work from Lipton's laboratory has shown that this process can be triggered by factors such as aging, inflammation and environmental exposures including air pollution and wildfire smoke. When large numbers of proteins are affected, the resulting disruption, described as a "SNO-STORM," can interfere with normal cellular function.

Researchers have linked this phenomenon to several diseases, including cancer, Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease.

Pinpointing the Alzheimer's Switch

For the new study, Lipton's team focused on STING because earlier research had already connected it to inflammation in Alzheimer's disease.

Led by postdoctoral researcher Lauren Carnevale, the group worked with Scripps Research Professor John Yates III, a leading expert in mass spectrometry and holder of the John Lytton Young Endowed Chair. Together, they identified the exact location on STING where S-nitrosylation occurs.

Their investigation revealed that the reaction targets a specific component of the protein called cysteine 148. Once this site becomes S-nitrosylated, STING begins clustering into larger complexes that activate inflammatory responses.

The researchers detected high levels of this altered form, known as SNO-STING, in postmortem brain tissue from people with Alzheimer's disease. Elevated levels were also found in human brain immune cells grown in the laboratory and exposed to Alzheimer's related proteins, as well as in a mouse model of the disease.

A Self Sustaining Cycle of Inflammation

The team also discovered that protein clumps commonly associated with Alzheimer's disease, including amyloid-beta and alpha-synuclein, can trigger the S-nitrosylation of STING.

This finding suggests that inflammation may become trapped in a repeating cycle. Protein aggregates, together with aging and environmental factors, may spark inflammation that generates nitric oxide. That nitric oxide can then promote S-nitrosylation of STING, which drives even more inflammation and further amplifies the process.

To test whether interrupting this cycle could help, the researchers engineered a version of STING that lacked cysteine 148 and therefore could not undergo S-nitrosylation.

When this modified protein was introduced into a mouse model of Alzheimer's disease, brain immune cells showed much lower levels of inflammation. Just as importantly, the synapses that connect nerve cells were protected from deterioration. Preserving these connections is strongly associated with protection against the cognitive decline seen in dementia.

A Potential New Treatment Strategy

"What makes this target particularly promising is that we can quiet the pathological overactivation of STING without shutting down the normal immune response," says Lipton. "You still need STING to protect yourself from infections, and when we target cysteine 148, we're not blocking the entire molecule; we're just preventing STING from becoming overactivated."

The research team is now developing small molecules designed to block cysteine 148 and plans to evaluate them in future preclinical studies.

In addition to Lipton, Carnevale and Yates, authors of the study, "Redox regulation of neuroinflammatory pathways contributes to damage in Alzheimer's disease brain," are Piu Banerjee, Xu Zhang, Jazmin Navarro, Charlene K Raspur, Parth Patel, Tomohiro Nakamura, Emily Schahrer, Henry Scott, Nhi Lang, Jolene K. Diedrich and Amanda J. Roberts of Scripps Research.

This work was supported in part by the National Institutes of Health (R35 AG071734, U01 AG088679, RF1 AG057409, R01 AG078756, R01 AG056259, R01 DA048882, DP1 DA041722 and R01 AG077046), and the U.S. Department of Defense/U.S. Department of the Army (AR230101).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough identifying a molecular mechanism fueling brain inflammation in Alzheimer's disease, which offers a promising new therapeutic target. The discovery has broad implications for millions affected by Alzheimer's and provides detailed context on the mechanism, experimental validation, and potential treatment strategies.
articles.personality_title: Scientists identify molecular switch driving harmful brain inflammation in Alzheimer’s
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Alzheimer’s disease causes memory loss and other problems because brain cells get damaged. Scientists know that in Alzheimer’s, the brain’s immune system gets stuck in a harmful “on” state, causing inflammation that damages connections between nerve cells.

**What happened** – Researchers at Scripps Research found a protein called STING that acts like a switch for this inflammation. In Alzheimer’s, STING changes chemically through a process called S-nitrosylation, making it stay active too long and cause damage. When they blocked this change in mice, inflammation went down and brain cell connections were protected. The same changes were seen in human brain samples and cells grown in the lab.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows a specific cause of harmful inflammation in Alzheimer’s and offers a new target for treatments. Unlike completely turning off the immune system, blocking STING’s overactivation helps reduce damage without stopping the brain’s ability to fight infections.

**What’s next step** – The scientists are now working on developing small drugs that can block the harmful change on STING. These will be tested in future lab studies to see if they can safely reduce inflammation and protect brain cells in Alzheimer’s.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers have found a chemical switch in the brain’s immune system that drives damaging inflammation in Alzheimer’s and are developing treatments to block it safely.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques identifient l’interrupteur moléculaire responsable de l’inflammation cérébrale dans la maladie d’Alzheimer
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La maladie d’Alzheimer provoque des pertes de mémoire et d’autres troubles car les cellules du cerveau sont endommagées. On sait que dans cette maladie, le système immunitaire du cerveau reste bloqué en état d’activation nuisible, provoquant une inflammation qui abîme les connexions entre les cellules nerveuses.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Scripps Research ont découvert une protéine appelée STING qui agit comme un interrupteur pour cette inflammation. Dans la maladie d’Alzheimer, STING subit une modification chimique appelée S-nitrosylation, qui la rend trop active et cause des dégâts. En bloquant cette modification chez la souris, l’inflammation a diminué et les connexions des cellules cérébrales ont été protégées. Les mêmes modifications ont été observées dans des échantillons de cerveau humain et des cellules cultivées en laboratoire.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre une cause précise de l’inflammation nuisible dans Alzheimer et offre une nouvelle cible pour des traitements. Au lieu d’éteindre complètement le système immunitaire, bloquer la surexcitation de STING réduit les dommages sans empêcher le cerveau de se défendre contre les infections.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques développent maintenant de petites molécules capables de bloquer cette modification de STING. Ces molécules seront testées en laboratoire pour vérifier si elles peuvent réduire l’inflammation en toute sécurité et protéger les cellules cérébrales dans la maladie d’Alzheimer.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs ont trouvé un interrupteur chimique dans le système immunitaire du cerveau qui cause l’inflammation nuisible dans Alzheimer et développent des traitements pour le bloquer en toute sécurité.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos identifican el interruptor molecular que impulsa la inflamación cerebral en el Alzheimer
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La enfermedad de Alzheimer causa pérdida de memoria y otros problemas porque las células del cerebro se dañan. Se sabe que en el Alzheimer, el sistema inmunitario del cerebro se queda atascado en un estado dañino, causando inflamación que daña las conexiones entre las células nerviosas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Scripps Research encontraron una proteína llamada STING que funciona como un interruptor para esta inflamación. En el Alzheimer, STING cambia químicamente mediante un proceso llamado S-nitrosilación, lo que la mantiene demasiado activa y causa daño. Al bloquear este cambio en ratones, la inflamación disminuyó y las conexiones entre células cerebrales se protegieron. Los mismos cambios se observaron en muestras de cerebros humanos y en células cultivadas en laboratorio.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra una causa específica de la inflamación dañina en Alzheimer y ofrece un nuevo objetivo para tratamientos. En lugar de apagar todo el sistema inmunitario, bloquear la sobreactivación de STING ayuda a reducir el daño sin detener la capacidad del cerebro para defenderse.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos están desarrollando pequeñas moléculas que pueden bloquear este cambio dañino en STING. Estas serán probadas en estudios de laboratorio para ver si pueden reducir la inflamación y proteger las células cerebrales de forma segura en Alzheimer.

**Una frase clave** – Los investigadores encontraron un interruptor químico en el sistema inmunitario del cerebro que causa inflamación dañina en Alzheimer y están creando tratamientos para bloquearlo de manera segura.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a serene brain-shaped landscape where glowing, interconnected neural pathways are gently shielded by protective, softly glowing molecular chains symbolizing the STING protein, with delicate golden locks representing the blocked harmful chemical modification, surrounded by subtle swirling mists of inflammation being calmed and dissipated.

articles.title: A new drug just cleared hepatitis B in 1 in 5 patients
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM A clinical trial published May 28 in the New England Journal of Medicine reports that a new drug called bepirovirsen achieved a functional cure in approximately one in five patients with chronic hepatitis B. That number matters. The current standard of care achieves a functional cure in around three […]
The post A new drug just cleared hepatitis B in 1 in 5 patients first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

A clinical trial published May 28 in the New England Journal of Medicine reports that a new drug called bepirovirsen achieved a functional cure in approximately one in five patients with chronic hepatitis B.

That number matters. The current standard of care achieves a functional cure in around three percent of patients, and only after eight to 10 years of treatment.

The trial

The B-Well trial enrolled 1,838 adults across 29 countries. Participants received either a weekly injection of bepirovirsen or a placebo for six months, alongside their existing antiviral medication. Among those on the drug, 19 percent achieved a functional cure: their immune systems kept the virus under control without medication for more than six months. In the subgroup with the lowest viral surface antigen levels at the start, the cure rate reached 26 percent.

“We’ve not had a treatment come close to this level of cure,” says Seng Gee Lim, a study co-author and director of hepatology at the National University Health System in Singapore. “I think my patients will be extremely delighted to have this treatment available.”

Anna Suk-Fong Lok, director of clinical hepatology at the University of Michigan Medical School, reviewed the trial as an independent expert. “After many failed trials in the last 10 years, the B-Well Trials’ results provide hope that functional cure for hepatitis B is feasible,” she said.

How the drug works

Bepirovirsen targets the virus two ways. It binds to viral messenger RNA to block production of viral proteins. It also appears to be taken up by macrophages, the immune system’s front-line white blood cells, triggering an immune response against hepatitis B. That makes it function more as an immunomodulator than a standard antiviral, Lok explains: instead of just suppressing the virus indefinitely from the outside, it helps the body’s own defenses take back control.

Who it helps, and who it doesn’t

The results had clear limits. The trial excluded people with cirrhosis, HIV coinfection, or severe disease. The drug worked best in patients whose hepatitis B was already better controlled at the outset.

“That reinforces a critical point: new treatments will only change lives if people are diagnosed early, regularly monitored, and connected to care,” says Jane Davies, an infectious disease specialist at the Royal Darwin Hospital in Australia and director of Hepatitis Australia.

Why this disease needs a breakthrough

More than 240 million people are living with chronic hepatitis B worldwide. Only 13 percent of them know it. The virus can silently damage the liver for years with no symptoms, or only mild ones: fatigue, stomach pain, joint discomfort. Hepatitis B is the second most potent carcinogen after tobacco, and over time it can cause liver scarring, liver failure, and liver cancer. More than one million people die from its complications each year.

“Hepatitis B is a silent killer,” says Lok.

Existing antivirals, which Lok describes as “cheap, safe, and effective,” keep the virus suppressed but rarely clear it. Patients typically stay on medication for life. The hepatitis B vaccine, introduced in 1981, offers close to 100 percent protection and has cut new infections by more than 90 percent in many countries. In the US, birth dosing has averted more than 90,100 potential childhood deaths since 1994. But treatment gaps remain serious, especially in sub-Saharan Africa and the western Pacific, where infection rates are highest and vaccination coverage is lowest. Geoffrey Dusheiko, a hepatologist at the Royal Free Hospital and University College London, calls it “appalling inequity.”

What comes next

GSK has submitted the trial data to regulators in the United States, Canada, Europe, Japan, and China, with approval decisions expected this year. Potential side effects include changes to platelet counts and kidney function, and injection site reactions, all requiring monitoring.

“These are exciting new developments,” Davies says, “but the immediate priority remains the same: find people living with hepatitis B, connect them with culturally safe care, and support them before liver disease or liver cancer develops. This does not have to remain a silent epidemic.”

Source study: New England Journal of Medicine— Phase 3 results of bepirovirsen treatment for chronic Hepatitis B virus infection

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough with a new drug, bepirovirsen, achieving a functional cure in about 20% of chronic hepatitis B patients, a substantial improvement over current treatments. This breakthrough has broad global health implications given the large number of people affected by hepatitis B worldwide and the severe consequences of the disease. The article provides detailed context about the trial, drug mechanism, and the impact on public health, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: New drug cures hepatitis B in 1 in 5 patients after six months
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Hepatitis B is a virus that infects the liver and affects more than 240 million people worldwide. Most people don’t know they have it because symptoms can be mild or absent. Current treatments only control the virus and rarely cure it, often requiring patients to take medicine for life.

**What happened** – A new drug called bepirovirsen was tested in a large clinical trial involving 1,838 adults from 29 countries. Participants took the drug or a placebo once a week for six months, along with their usual antiviral medication. About 19% of those taking bepirovirsen achieved a “functional cure,” meaning their immune system kept the virus under control without medicine for more than six months. Patients with lower virus levels at the start had a cure rate of 26%.

**Impact** – This is the first treatment to reach such a high cure rate in a short time, compared to the current 3% cure rate after many years. The drug works by helping the body’s immune cells fight the virus instead of just blocking it. This breakthrough offers hope for millions living with hepatitis B, especially if they are diagnosed early and receive proper care.

**What’s next step** – The company behind bepirovirsen is asking health authorities in several countries to approve the drug. Doctors will need to watch for possible side effects like changes in blood cells or kidney function. Meanwhile, experts stress the importance of finding people with hepatitis B early and connecting them to treatment to prevent serious liver damage.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new drug called bepirovirsen can clear hepatitis B in about one in five patients within six months by boosting the immune system’s ability to control the virus.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau médicament guérit l’hépatite B chez 1 patient sur 5 en six mois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’hépatite B est un virus qui infecte le foie et touche plus de 240 millions de personnes dans le monde. La plupart ne savent pas qu’elles sont infectées car les symptômes sont souvent légers ou absents. Les traitements actuels contrôlent seulement le virus et guérissent rarement, obligeant souvent les patients à prendre des médicaments à vie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un nouveau médicament appelé bépirovirsène a été testé lors d’un grand essai clinique avec 1 838 adultes dans 29 pays. Les participants recevaient une injection hebdomadaire du médicament ou un placebo pendant six mois, en plus de leur traitement antiviral habituel. Environ 19 % des patients sous bépirovirsène ont obtenu une « guérison fonctionnelle », c’est-à-dire que leur système immunitaire contrôlait le virus sans médicament pendant plus de six mois. Le taux de guérison atteignait 26 % chez ceux avec un faible niveau de virus au départ.

**Impact** – C’est le premier traitement atteignant un taux de guérison aussi élevé en si peu de temps, comparé aux 3 % actuels après plusieurs années. Le médicament agit en aidant les cellules immunitaires à combattre le virus, plutôt qu’en le bloquant seulement. Cette avancée offre un espoir pour des millions de personnes vivant avec l’hépatite B, surtout si elles sont diagnostiquées tôt et soignées correctement.

**Prochaine étape** – L’entreprise demande l’autorisation des autorités sanitaires dans plusieurs pays pour utiliser le médicament. Les médecins devront surveiller d’éventuels effets secondaires comme des changements dans les cellules sanguines ou la fonction rénale. En attendant, les experts insistent sur l’importance de détecter tôt les personnes infectées et de les relier aux soins pour éviter des dommages graves au foie.

**En une phrase** – Un nouveau médicament, la bépirovirsène, peut guérir l’hépatite B chez environ un patient sur cinq en six mois en renforçant la capacité du système immunitaire à contrôler le virus.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo medicamento cura hepatitis B en 1 de cada 5 pacientes en seis meses
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La hepatitis B es un virus que infecta el hígado y afecta a más de 240 millones de personas en el mundo. Muchas no saben que están infectadas porque los síntomas suelen ser leves o no aparecen. Los tratamientos actuales solo controlan el virus y rara vez lo curan, obligando a los pacientes a tomar medicinas de por vida.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo medicamento llamado bepirovirsen fue probado en un gran ensayo clínico con 1,838 adultos de 29 países. Los participantes recibieron una inyección semanal del medicamento o un placebo durante seis meses, junto con su medicación antiviral habitual. Alrededor del 19 % de quienes recibieron bepirovirsen lograron una “cura funcional”, lo que significa que su sistema inmunológico mantuvo el virus bajo control sin medicinas por más de seis meses. En pacientes con niveles bajos del virus al inicio, la cura fue del 26 %.

**Impacto** – Este es el primer tratamiento que alcanza una tasa de cura tan alta en tan poco tiempo, comparado con el 3 % actual tras muchos años. El medicamento ayuda a las células inmunes a combatir el virus en vez de solo bloquearlo. Este avance da esperanza a millones que viven con hepatitis B, especialmente si se diagnostican temprano y reciben atención adecuada.

**Próximo paso** – La compañía que desarrolló bepirovirsen está solicitando la aprobación del medicamento en varios países. Los médicos deberán vigilar posibles efectos secundarios como cambios en las células sanguíneas o función renal. Mientras tanto, los expertos destacan la importancia de encontrar a las personas con hepatitis B a tiempo y conectarlas con tratamiento para evitar daños graves en el hígado.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo medicamento llamado bepirovirsen puede curar la hepatitis B en aproximadamente uno de cada cinco pacientes en seis meses al fortalecer la capacidad del sistema inmunológico para controlar el virus.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a strong, protective white shield gently enveloping a stylized, glowing liver symbol made of soft red and orange hues, with delicate, translucent blue strands representing viral messenger RNA being gently blocked and broken apart around it, set against a calm, neutral background with warm, natural tones.

articles.title: Intermittent fasting triggers surprising changes in the brain
articles.summary: Losing weight may involve rewiring the gut and the brain at the same time. In a study of obese adults, an intermittent fasting-style diet led to significant weight loss, healthier metabolic markers, and notable shifts in gut bacteria. Brain scans also revealed changes in regions tied to appetite, cravings, and self-control. The results suggest the gut microbiome and brain may work together to influence weight-loss success.
articles.content:
More than one billion people worldwide now live with obesity, a condition that raises the risk of cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and several types of cancer. Yet losing weight and keeping it off can be extremely difficult. The body does not simply respond to fewer calories in a straightforward way. Signals from the gut, hormones, metabolism, and the brain can all influence hunger, cravings, and weight regain.

One approach that has drawn growing interest is intermittent energy restriction (IER), a form of dieting in which periods of reduced calorie intake are followed by periods of more typical eating. Research published in 2023 suggests that this strategy may do more than reduce body weight. It may also shift the relationship between gut bacteria and brain activity in ways that are closely tied to appetite and food behavior.

"Here we show that an IER diet changes the human brain-gut-microbiome axis. The observed changes in the gut microbiome and in the activity in addition-related brain regions during and after weight loss are highly dynamic and coupled over time," said last author Dr. Qiang Zeng, a researcher at the Health Management Institute of the PLA General Hospital in Beijing.

Intermittent fasting and the brain

To explore what happens inside the body during weight loss, the researchers studied 25 adults with obesity in China. The volunteers, who were about 27 years old on average, had a BMI between 28 and 45.

The team used several tools to track changes over time. Stool samples were analyzed with metagenomics to measure the composition of the gut microbiome. Blood tests were used to monitor metabolic and physiological changes. The researchers also used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to examine activity in brain regions involved in appetite, emotion, attention, learning, inhibition, and reward.

"A healthy, balanced gut microbiome is critical for energy homeostasis and maintaining normal weight. In contrast, an abnormal gut microbiome can change our eating behavior by affecting certain brain area involved in addiction," explained coauthor Dr. Yongli Li from the Department of Health Management of Henan Provincial People's Hospital in Henan, China.

A carefully controlled weight loss program

The study began with a 32 day high controlled fasting phase. During this period, participants received meals designed by a dietitian. Their calorie intake was gradually reduced in steps until it reached about one quarter of their basic energy needs.

This was followed by a 30 day low controlled fasting phase. During this stage, participants were given a list of recommended foods rather than fully prepared meals. Those who followed the plan exactly would consume 500 calories per day for women and 600 calories per day for men.

By the end of the intervention, participants had lost an average of 7.6 kilograms, equal to about 7.8% of their starting body weight. They also had reductions in body fat and waist circumference.

The metabolic improvements extended beyond weight. Blood pressure fell, as did fasting plasma glucose, total cholesterol, HDL, LDL, and the activity of key liver enzymes. According to the researchers, these changes suggest that intermittent energy restriction may help reduce obesity related problems such as hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and liver dysfunction.

Brain and gut changes moved together

The researchers found that the weight loss program was linked to lower activity in several brain regions involved in appetite and addiction related behavior. These changes may help explain why dieting affects not only body size, but also food cravings, self control, and the drive to eat.

At the same time, the gut microbiome shifted. The abundance of Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, Parabacteroides distasonis, and Bacterokles uniformis rose sharply. Escherichia coli decreased.

Further analysis suggested that certain microbes were connected with activity in specific brain areas. The abundance of E. coli, Coprococcus comes, and Eubacterium hallii was negatively associated with activity in the brain's left orbital inferior frontal gyrus, a region involved in executive function and willpower during weight loss.

Other bacteria showed the opposite pattern. P. distasonis and Flavonifractor plautii were positively linked with brain regions involved in attention, motor inhibition, emotion, and learning.

These findings point to a striking possibility: as people lose weight, the gut microbiome and the brain may change together. The study cannot prove whether gut bacteria drive the brain changes, whether the brain drives microbial changes, or whether another factor influences both. Still, the results add to evidence that weight control is not just a matter of willpower or calories. It may involve a changing biological conversation between the gut and the brain.

A two way conversation inside the body

"The gut microbiome is thought to communicate with the brain in a complex, two-directional way. The microbiome produces neurotransmitters and neurotoxins which access the brain through nerves and the blood circulation. In return the brain controls eating behavior, while nutrients from our diet change the composition of the gut microbiome," said coauthor Dr. Xiaoning Wang from the Institute of Geriatrics of the PLA General Hospital.

This two way communication may help explain why obesity is so difficult to treat. Hunger, cravings, mood, reward, and metabolism are all shaped by biological signals. The gut microbiome can produce compounds that influence inflammation, metabolism, and nervous system activity. The brain, in turn, helps regulate food choices and eating behavior.

The 2023 findings suggest that successful weight loss may involve changes across this entire system rather than in one isolated organ.

What later research adds

Research published after the 2023 study has continued to support the idea that fasting can influence the gut microbiome, although the evidence remains complex. A 2024 systematic review of human studies found that intermittent fasting appears to affect gut microbial richness, diversity, and composition. However, the authors also noted that results varied widely between studies, and more research is needed to determine which changes are truly beneficial for health.

Another 2024 clinical study compared intermittent fasting combined with protein pacing to continuous calorie restriction in adults with overweight or obesity. Both diets reduced calorie intake, but the fasting and protein pacing group showed greater weight loss and more pronounced shifts in the gut microbiome. The researchers reported increases in microbes and metabolic signals associated with improved body composition and fat loss.

Together, these later findings strengthen the broader picture: fasting based interventions may reshape the gut microbiome in meaningful ways. However, they also show that the details matter. The type of fasting, calorie intake, protein intake, fiber intake, meal timing, and individual biology may all influence the outcome.

The next question for weight loss research

The original 2023 study was small and correlational, so it cannot show cause and effect. It also focused on a specific group of participants and a short term intervention. Larger and longer studies will be needed to determine whether certain microbes or brain regions can reliably predict who will lose weight, who will keep it off, and which diets work best for different people.

Coauthor Dr. Liming Wang, likewise from the Health Management Institute in Beijing, said: "The next question to be answered is the precise mechanism by which the gut microbiome and the brain communicate in obese people, including during weight loss. What specific gut microbiome and brain regions are critical for successful weight loss and maintaining a healthy weight?"

For now, the research offers a more detailed view of what may happen during intermittent fasting. Weight loss may not be limited to shrinking fat stores. It may also involve a synchronized shift in gut bacteria, metabolism, and brain activity that changes how the body responds to food.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific study showing that intermittent fasting not only leads to weight loss but also triggers beneficial changes in the gut microbiome and brain regions related to appetite and self-control. This research has broad implications for addressing obesity, a major global health issue, by revealing a complex biological mechanism that could improve weight loss strategies and metabolic health. The article provides detailed context, study methods, and future research directions, demonstrating substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: Intermittent fasting changes gut bacteria and brain to help weight loss
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – More than one billion people around the world struggle with obesity, which increases the risk of serious diseases like heart problems and diabetes. Losing weight is hard because many factors, including the brain and gut, affect hunger and cravings.

**What happened** – A study in China followed 25 adults with obesity who tried an intermittent fasting diet for about two months. This diet involved periods of eating very few calories followed by normal eating. Researchers checked their gut bacteria, blood health, and brain activity using special scans before, during, and after the diet.

**Impact** – Participants lost around 7.6 kilograms and showed better blood pressure, cholesterol, and liver health. The brain scans showed less activity in areas linked to cravings and addiction, while gut bacteria changed in ways connected to better self-control and attention. This suggests losing weight might involve the gut and brain working together, not just eating less.

**What's next step** – More research is needed with larger groups to understand exactly how gut bacteria and brain areas communicate during weight loss. Scientists want to find out which microbes and brain regions are most important to help people keep weight off long-term.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Intermittent fasting can help weight loss by changing gut bacteria and brain activity linked to appetite and self-control, showing weight loss is a complex process involving both body and brain.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le jeûne intermittent modifie les bactéries intestinales et le cerveau pour faciliter la perte de poids
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Plus d’un milliard de personnes dans le monde souffrent d’obésité, ce qui augmente le risque de maladies graves comme les problèmes cardiaques et le diabète. Perdre du poids est difficile car plusieurs facteurs, dont le cerveau et l’intestin, influencent la faim et les envies.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une étude en Chine a suivi 25 adultes obèses qui ont suivi un régime de jeûne intermittent pendant environ deux mois. Ce régime alterne des périodes de très faible apport calorique avec des périodes d’alimentation normale. Les chercheurs ont analysé leurs bactéries intestinales, leur santé sanguine et l’activité cérébrale grâce à des scans avant, pendant et après le régime.

**Impact** – Les participants ont perdu environ 7,6 kilogrammes et ont montré une amélioration de leur tension artérielle, leur cholestérol et leur santé du foie. Les scans cérébraux ont révélé une activité réduite dans les zones liées aux envies et à l’addiction, tandis que les bactéries intestinales ont changé de façon liée à un meilleur contrôle de soi et à une meilleure attention. Cela suggère que la perte de poids implique une collaboration entre l’intestin et le cerveau, pas seulement une réduction des calories.

**Prochaine étape** – D’autres recherches avec un plus grand nombre de participants sont nécessaires pour comprendre comment les bactéries intestinales et les zones du cerveau communiquent lors de la perte de poids. Les scientifiques veulent identifier les microbes et régions cérébrales clés pour aider à maintenir la perte de poids.

**Phrase clé** – Le jeûne intermittent favorise la perte de poids en modifiant les bactéries intestinales et l’activité cérébrale liée à l’appétit et au contrôle de soi, montrant que la perte de poids est un processus complexe impliquant corps et cerveau.
articles.personality_title_es: El ayuno intermitente cambia bacterias intestinales y cerebro para ayudar a perder peso
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Más de mil millones de personas en el mundo tienen obesidad, lo que aumenta el riesgo de enfermedades graves como problemas cardíacos y diabetes. Perder peso es difícil porque muchos factores, incluido el cerebro y el intestino, afectan el hambre y los antojos.

**Qué pasó** – Un estudio en China siguió a 25 adultos con obesidad que hicieron una dieta de ayuno intermitente durante unos dos meses. Esta dieta alterna períodos de comer muy pocas calorías con períodos de alimentación normal. Los investigadores analizaron sus bacterias intestinales, salud sanguínea y actividad cerebral con escáneres antes, durante y después de la dieta.

**Impacto** – Los participantes perdieron alrededor de 7.6 kilogramos y mejoraron su presión arterial, colesterol y salud del hígado. Los escáneres cerebrales mostraron menos actividad en áreas relacionadas con los antojos y la adicción, mientras que las bacterias intestinales cambiaron de manera que se relaciona con mejor autocontrol y atención. Esto sugiere que perder peso puede involucrar la colaboración entre el intestino y el cerebro, no solo comer menos.

**Próximo paso** – Se necesitan más estudios con grupos más grandes para entender cómo se comunican las bacterias intestinales y las áreas del cerebro durante la pérdida de peso. Los científicos quieren descubrir qué microbios y regiones cerebrales son clave para ayudar a mantener el peso.

**Frase clave** – El ayuno intermitente puede ayudar a perder peso al cambiar las bacterias intestinales y la actividad cerebral relacionada con el apetito y el autocontrol, mostrando que la pérdida de peso es un proceso complejo que involucra cuerpo y cerebro.
articles.image_prompt: A harmonious, detailed painting of a human brain and a stylized gut intertwined by glowing, flowing neural and microbial pathways, with vibrant clusters of distinct gut bacteria shapes shifting in color and form along the connection, set against a soft, warm background of natural earth tones symbolizing balance and transformation.

articles.title: 'Animal medicine': Therapy donkeys help patients at French psychiatric hospital
articles.summary: Patients staying in a psychiatric hospital near Paris have been singing the praises of a novel treatment: spending time with therapy donkeys. Experts and patients say caring for the animals, which are known for their calm and social nature, helps improve emotional regulation, communication, social interaction and self-esteem.
articles.content:
A patient with mental health conditions participates in a therapy session involving donkeys at a psychiatric hospital in Neuilly-sur-Marne, in the eastern suburbs of Paris, on May 29, 2026.

Therapy donkeys are helping patients with mental health conditions recover in a psychiatric hospital unit outside Paris that's unique to France.

The 19th century farm buildings and wooded surroundings are a haven within the Ville-Evrard hospital complex in Neuilly-sur-Marne. On Friday, patients took the five donkeys for a walk and cared for them. Some confidently lifted their hooves to remove dirt. Many ended the session with a hug.

“When you take medication that helps you relax … it’s exactly the same,” said Nathalie, a 60-year-old patient. She and others were identified by their first names only to protect their privacy.

“I’d call it animal medicine,” she said. “It brings relief. You stop thinking about everything else."

Patients attend the sessions free of charge as part of their treatment, which is funded by France’s public health system.

Participants are usually paired with a donkey – Nono, Pitou, Oscar, Manolo or Malraux. Over time, they become familiar with each other’s personalities.

Audrey Seffar, a nurse at the animal therapy unit, said Nathalie's progress after only a few sessions was significant.

“At first, she wouldn’t get out of the cart (provided for people with physical difficulties). But little by little, with encouragement, she did," Seffar said. "The animal serves as a mediator. It’s such an extraordinary one that today she was able to leave the cart and stand beside her donkey."

Patients with mental health conditions, staff members and volunteers, participate in a therapy session involving donkeys in Neuilly-sur-Marne on May 29, 2026. © Thomas Padilla, AP

Another patient, Jérôme, 52, said the programme helps reduce loneliness.

“Talking with people, taking part in activities I wouldn’t normally do, it helps me in my daily life,” he said.

He added: “It helps you break away from the routine of treatment and medication. Staying at home isn’t good for me.”

The first donkeys arrived at Ville-Evrard hospital in 2016 as part of a project launched by Ermelinda and François Hadey.

Read moreBone appétit: Paris dogs lap up treats at canine-friendly cafés

Ermelinda, a nurse specializing in psychiatry, strongly believed in animal therapy benefits and thought donkeys, known for their calm and social nature, would be perfect. Her husband learned how to train donkeys for therapy work. Some of the animals were adopted through shelters after experiencing neglect or mistreatment.

“A donkey is very intelligent. It understands things very quickly, but you have to explain slowly,” François Hadey said. “Donkeys are calm, serene animals that are generally close to people. Once they’re involved in these interactions, they connect very well with patients. They’re emotional sponges.”

A patient with mental health conditions cleans a donkey's eyes during the animal therapy session in the eastern suburbs of Paris, on May 29, 2026. © Thomas Padilla, AP

Since 2022, the animal therapy programme has had official status as a health care unit in the hospital, allowing it to employ three full-time nurses. Volunteers with a nonprofit group help care for the animals.

The programme has expanded to include guinea pigs, chickens, doves, goats, turtles and rabbits. Sessions are tailored to people’s needs and preferences, and smaller animals can be brought to hospital rooms.

Alicia Fabi, an 18-year-old nursing student, said the activity gives patients a chance to leave the hospital environment.

“Every time we come back from the activity, they say they feel good, calm and relaxed, and that they enjoyed the outing. That’s really positive,” she said.

Walking together also allows patients and health workers to develop a deeper relationship.

“We talk about many different things, their illness, their lives and just about everything else. We don’t focus only on the illness because we don’t want them dwelling on it all the time,” Fabi said.

Health workers say the sessions are designed as therapeutic interventions for living with anxiety, depression, autism, schizophrenia or other conditions. Staff said they can help improve emotional regulation, communication, social interaction and self-esteem.

“Everything we do with the animals allows us to work with the patient,” Ermelinda Hadey said. “We work on feeding the animal, which helps us address the patient’s own eating habits. We work on the animal’s hygiene, and by mirror effect, we work on the patient’s hygiene as well.”

Patients with mental health conditions participate in a therapy session involving donkeys in Neuilly-sur-Marne. © Thomas Padilla, AP

Many patients take intensive treatments, including antipsychotic medications or sedatives, which can make it difficult to find the motivation to participate in activities, she said. That’s where the relationship to donkeys and other animals play a role, she stressed.

“It does not replace a doctor or a medical prescription, but it can help patients regain confidence and a sense of self-worth," Hadey said.

She said more scientific evaluation is needed. They would like animal therapy to be formally recognised by the psychiatric community as a complementary form of care.

“To do that, we need research. We have plenty of accounts from patients ... Caregivers who accompany them see the benefits every day as well. But doctors have so many other responsibilities that they don’t necessarily witness it firsthand,” she said.

At the end of Friday’s session, as patients chatted, a nurse summed up the program’s appeal: “Donkeys are my best colleagues.”

(FRANCE 24 with AP)
articles.source: Health: news, videos, reports and analysis - France 24
articles.reasoning: The article describes a novel and effective animal-assisted therapy program using donkeys to help psychiatric patients improve emotional regulation, communication, social interaction, and self-esteem. The therapy has measurable benefits for mental health patients, is integrated into a public health system, and impacts a broad group of people with mental health conditions, making it a significant and inspiring health innovation.
articles.personality_title: Therapy donkeys help patients heal at French psychiatric hospital
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – At a psychiatric hospital near Paris, patients with mental health conditions often face challenges like anxiety, depression, and loneliness. Traditional treatments include medication and therapy, but finding ways to improve patients' daily lives remains important.

**What happened** – Since 2016, the Ville-Evrard hospital in Neuilly-sur-Marne has been using donkeys as part of therapy sessions. Patients spend time caring for and walking the donkeys, which are calm and social animals. The program grew to become an official health care unit in 2022, with full-time nurses and volunteers helping. The therapy also includes other animals like guinea pigs and rabbits.

**Impact** – Patients say the donkeys help them feel calmer and more confident. For example, one patient who initially could not leave a cart was encouraged to stand beside her donkey after a few sessions. The animals help improve emotional control, communication, social skills, and self-esteem. The therapy offers a break from medication routines and helps patients connect with others and their surroundings.

**What's next step** – The hospital team wants more scientific research to prove how effective animal therapy is. They hope this will lead to wider acceptance in the medical community and more support for using animals as a helpful part of mental health care.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Caring for therapy donkeys is helping psychiatric patients improve their mental health by boosting confidence and social connection in a unique hospital program outside Paris.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des ânes thérapeutiques aident les patients dans un hôpital psychiatrique français
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans un hôpital psychiatrique près de Paris, les patients souffrant de troubles mentaux rencontrent souvent des difficultés comme l’anxiété, la dépression et la solitude. Les traitements traditionnels incluent les médicaments et la thérapie, mais il est important de trouver des moyens d’améliorer leur quotidien.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Depuis 2016, l’hôpital Ville-Evrard à Neuilly-sur-Marne utilise des ânes dans ses séances de thérapie. Les patients passent du temps à s’occuper des ânes calmes et sociables, en les promenant et en les soignant. Le programme est devenu une unité officielle de soins en 2022, avec des infirmiers à plein temps et des bénévoles. D’autres animaux comme des cochons d’Inde et des lapins participent aussi.

**Impact** – Les patients disent que les ânes les aident à se sentir plus calmes et confiants. Par exemple, une patiente qui ne pouvait pas quitter un chariot a réussi à se tenir debout à côté de son âne après quelques séances. Les animaux améliorent le contrôle émotionnel, la communication, les compétences sociales et l’estime de soi. La thérapie offre une pause dans les traitements médicamenteux et aide les patients à se connecter aux autres.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe souhaite plus de recherches scientifiques pour prouver l’efficacité de la thérapie animale. Ils espèrent que cela permettra une meilleure reconnaissance médicale et plus de soutien pour utiliser les animaux dans les soins de santé mentale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – S’occuper d’ânes thérapeutiques aide les patients psychiatriques à améliorer leur santé mentale en renforçant leur confiance et leurs liens sociaux dans un programme unique près de Paris.
articles.personality_title_es: Burros terapéuticos ayudan a pacientes en hospital psiquiátrico francés
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En un hospital psiquiátrico cerca de París, los pacientes con problemas de salud mental enfrentan desafíos como ansiedad, depresión y soledad. Los tratamientos comunes incluyen medicamentos y terapia, pero es importante encontrar formas de mejorar su vida diaria.

**Qué pasó** – Desde 2016, el hospital Ville-Evrard en Neuilly-sur-Marne usa burros en sesiones de terapia. Los pacientes pasan tiempo cuidando y paseando a los burros, que son animales tranquilos y sociales. El programa se convirtió en una unidad oficial de salud en 2022, con enfermeros de tiempo completo y voluntarios ayudando. También participan otros animales como cobayas y conejos.

**Impacto** – Los pacientes dicen que los burros los ayudan a sentirse más tranquilos y seguros. Por ejemplo, una paciente que no podía bajarse de un carrito logró estar parada junto a su burro después de algunas sesiones. Los animales mejoran el control emocional, la comunicación, las habilidades sociales y la autoestima. La terapia ofrece un descanso de los medicamentos y ayuda a los pacientes a conectarse con otros.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo del hospital quiere más investigaciones científicas para demostrar la efectividad de la terapia con animales. Esperan que esto lleve a un mayor reconocimiento médico y más apoyo para usar animales como parte del cuidado de la salud mental.

**Resumen en una frase** – Cuidar burros terapéuticos está ayudando a pacientes psiquiátricos a mejorar su salud mental aumentando su confianza y conexión social en un programa único cerca de París.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of gentle donkeys calmly interacting with human-shaped silhouettes in a serene, rustic 19th-century farm courtyard surrounded by soft woods, where the silhouettes gently care for and walk alongside the donkeys, symbolizing healing and companionship through animal therapy.
